coredata.dc:title: Investigation on the synergistic effect and thermal properties of poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate encapsulated graphene quantum dot-paraffin hybrid nanofluid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraj, Vishnuprasad
Sreelekshmi, S.
Binoy, Mariya
George, Sony
Senthilkumar, Karthick
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel water-based hybrid nanofluid was developed using poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite (HEMA GQD-P). Graphene quantum dots (GQD) with an average size of 10 to 15 nm were synthesized by citric acid nucleation and the HEMA GQD-P nanocomposites were synthesized by an emulsion polymerization reaction. The emission spectra of GQD obtained using photo-luminescent spectroscopy confirm the occurrence of quantum confinement effect. The synthesis of HEMA GQD-P was carefully carried out to ensure its optimal properties. The synergistic effect due to high thermal conductivity of GQD and high latent heat capacity of paraffin were recognized in the enhanced properties of nanocomposite and hybrid nanofluid. The latent heat capacity of the synthesized nanocomposite was found to be 21.8 % higher than the pure paraffin. The prepared hybrid nanofluid possesses high dispersion stability at pH 7 and the thermal conductivity of the hybrid nanofluid enhances with an increase in nanoparticle concentration. A maximum thermal conductivity enhancement of 53 % (±2) was obtained for the hybrid nanofluid (0.3 vol%) at 30 °C. The present work reveals that the encapsulation of GQD-paraffin in a poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate shell creates nanocomposites with high heat transfer properties. The synergistic effect of the HEMA GQD-P enhances their potential in the preparation of hybrid nanofluids with enhanced properties for desired thermal management applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Paraffin
Hybrid nanofluid
Thermal conductivity
Latent heat capacity
Emulsion polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite suspension
Analysis of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid
Characterization of HEMA GQD-P composite particles
Thermo-physical properties of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis mechanism of graphene quantum dots using citric acid.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of HEMA coated Paraffin-Graphene quantum dots composite
UV absorption spectra of graphene quantum dots suspensions at various concentrations.
Photoluminescence emission spectra of graphene quantum dots suspension at various excitation wavelengths.
a) FTIR Spectra of paraffin and GQD nanoparticles and b) FTIR spectrum of HEMA-GQD-P nanocomposites.
Raman spectrum of GQD nanoparticles.
a) TEM image of graphene quantum dot particles and b) SEM images of HEMA GQD-P composite particles
TEM image of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles.
Differential Scanning Calorimetry curve of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles.
Effect of GQD-paraffin wt. ratio in HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite on latent heat capacity, thermal conductivity and phase change temperature of the nanocomposite.
Zeta potential of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid as a function of pH.
Photographic images of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid at a) day 1, and b) day 30 after preparation.
a) Thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid at various particle loadings and b) Effect of temperature on the thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid.
Viscosity and surface tension of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid at various particle loadings.
Effect of temperature on the viscosity of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid.
Effect of temperature on the surface tension of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid.
General thermal properties of the GQD, paraffin PCM and HEMA polymer.
Comparison of the HEMA GQD-P properties with similar innovative material.
Maximum uncertainty of all experimental properties.

head.ce:title: Investigation on the synergistic effect and thermal properties of poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate encapsulated graphene quantum dot-paraffin hybrid nanofluid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Saintgits College of Engineering, Kottayam-686532, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Saintgits College of Engineering, Kottayam – 686532

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology, Allahabad-211004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology, Allahabad - 211004

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Technology, Saintgits College of Engineering, Kottayam-686532
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Technology, Saintgits College of Engineering, Kottayam-686532

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel water-based hybrid nanofluid was developed using poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite (HEMA GQD-P). Graphene quantum dots (GQD) with an average size of 10 to 15 nm were synthesized by citric acid nucleation and the HEMA GQD-P nanocomposites were synthesized by an emulsion polymerization reaction. The emission spectra of GQD obtained using photo-luminescent spectroscopy confirm the occurrence of quantum confinement effect. The synthesis of HEMA GQD-P was carefully carried out to ensure its optimal properties. The synergistic effect due to high thermal conductivity of GQD and high latent heat capacity of paraffin were recognized in the enhanced properties of nanocomposite and hybrid nanofluid. The latent heat capacity of the synthesized nanocomposite was found to be 21.8 % higher than the pure paraffin. The prepared hybrid nanofluid possesses high dispersion stability at pH 7 and the thermal conductivity of the hybrid nanofluid enhances with an increase in nanoparticle concentration. A maximum thermal conductivity enhancement of 53 % (±2) was obtained for the hybrid nanofluid (0.3 vol%) at 30 °C. The present work reveals that the encapsulation of GQD-paraffin in a poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate shell creates nanocomposites with high heat transfer properties. The synergistic effect of the HEMA GQD-P enhances their potential in the preparation of hybrid nanofluids with enhanced properties for desired thermal management applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enhanced thermal conductivity and physico-chemical properties of heat transfer fluids can contribute to improved energy efficiency which is crucial in industries [1]. Thus, worldwide industry innovations are recently focusing on the miniaturization of heat transfer systems and adapting nanomaterials [2]. Nanomaterials have advantages such as larger relative surface area, high mobility, less particle momentum, better stability under suspended conditions, and improved thermal conductivity [3]. Finally, researchers came up with an effective strategy to improve the heat transfer rate of conventional heat transfer fluids by the dispersion of nanoparticles, thus known as nanofluids [4]. Nanofluids find applications as lubricants in engines, coolant and cutting fluids in manufacturing sectors, and hydraulic fluids in industrial automation [5].

Graphite, graphene, and carbon nanotubes are some of the conventional carbonaceous materials that are commonly used nanoparticles (NPs) with relatively higher thermal conductivity. Graphene is one of the most extensively studied carbon-based materials due to its outstanding thermal conductivity, which ranges between 2500 W/mK and 5000 W/mK [6]. Nanoparticles have a higher surface area per unit volume compared to larger particles. This increased surface area can enhance the interaction between the nanoparticles and the base fluid, potentially leading to improved thermal conductivity. Hence, researchers developed zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQD) with high specific surface area with tunable properties compared to graphene. GQDs have many novel properties including the quantum confinement effect (i.e., exhibit fluorescent properties when exposed to UV radiation), eco-friendly nature, non-toxicity, inertness, and thermal stability [7].

For the first time, the thermal and rheological properties of GQD-dispersed glycerol nanofluid were investigated by Goharshadi et al. [8]. They reported that the thermal conductivity of GQD nanofluid was greater than graphene nanofluid and the thermal conductivity of GQD nanofluid increased to 10 % compared to basefluid by an addition of just 2 % GQDs were loaded at 25 °C. Also, the thermal conductivity of GQDs-glycerol suspensions increases exponentially with an increase in temperature. Similarly, Ettefaghi et al. investigated the heat transfer properties of graphene quantum dots-water nanofluid. They claimed that the graphene quantum dots possess long-term stability within the base fluid compared to pristine graphene without using any chemical surfactants. By applying the same in a heat transfer system, they have observed about 6 and 17 % improvement of thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficient respectively compared to the basefluid at very low concentration [9].

Apart from the high thermal conductivity, an ideal nanofluid should possess good energy storage capacity. A single nanoparticle cannot exhibit all the properties required for a nanofluid. Here comes the use of hybrid nanofluids prepared by dispersing multi-type nanoparticles or composite nanomaterials in basefluid. The hybrid approach aims to exploit the synergistic effects of multiple nanoparticles to develop and further improve the multi-characteristics of the nanofluid [10]. Phase-changing materials (PCM) are deployed as a thermal energy storage medium to add the property to the nanofluid. PCMs are materials that can store the latent heat during solid−liquid transitions [11]. High latent heat, good melting point, low cost as well as good chemical and thermal stability are some of the preferred properties of a PCM. The combined effect of high thermal conductivity and latent heat capacity can be obtained by dispersing nanoparticle-added PCM composite known as nano-enhanced PCM in basefluid [12,13]. The improved thermal properties of nano-enhanced PCM nanofluids contribute to increased energy efficiency in various systems. By facilitating efficient heat transfer and storage, these nanofluids can help reduce energy consumption and enhance the overall performance of thermal systems. However, the leakage of PCM from the nanoparticles during the phase transition from solid to liquid state by absorbing heat restricts its widespread application in nanofluids. The leaked molten PCM solidifies and recombines to form aggregated large-sized irregular PCM solid in the basefluid which adversely affects its default properties [14].

To avoid the leakage of PCM to its surroundings, microencapsulation of PCM in a polymeric shell is highly desirable. These microcapsules ideally should provide a platform to store and release latent heat of the PCM without undergoing any physicochemical transformation of core (PCM) as well as shell (polymer) materials [15]. Several characteristics such as heat transfer efficiency, thermal conductivity, water dispersibility, and durability of the PCM capsules are dependent on the nature of shell materials [16]. The properties of the capsule shell can significantly influence the absorption, storage, and release efficiencies of the thermal energies of the PCM capsules. Microencapsulation results in higher heat transfer rates which are attributed to its higher surface area to volume ratio, low chemical reactivity, and its ability to withstand the change in volume during the phase change process. This results in the melting and solidification of the micro-encapsulated PCM rapidly [17]. Pickering emulsion polymerization is commonly used in the production of polymer coating on particles, providing a versatile and environmentally friendly method for creating a variety of polymer-based coatings [18].

Recently, Xu et al. investigated the performance of polymer microencapsulated N-eicosane phase change material suspension in a ground heat exchanger. They observed a drastic change in the heat transfer performance of the nanofluid compared to conventional coolant. They reported that the heat transfer coefficient of 25 wt% PCM nanofluid with an inlet temperature of 30 °C was 10.5 times that of water [19]. Ghasemi et al. studied the heat transfer performance and flow characteristics of poly-methyl methacrylate encapsulated paraffin composite PCM nanofluid in a pillow plate heat exchanger. The PCM nanofluid has increased the heat transfer coefficient by 6.2 % by using a 15 % concentration as a result of increasing the heat capacity. They also reported that PCM nanofluids contribute to better cooling performance and improved energy efficiency leading reduced energy consumption and operating costs of the system [20].

According to the nature of PCMs, they are classified as inorganic (metallic compounds and hydrated salts), organic (glycols, paraffin waxes, fatty acids, and alcohols), and eutectic mixtures of organic or inorganic PCM. Although inorganic PCMs have high latent heat capacity their application is limited due to their strong corrosive nature and high undercooling [21]. But organic PCMs have the advantages of high latent heat capacity, and a non-toxic and non-corrosive nature. Paraffin is the most commonly used organic PCM because of its low cost, availability, low melting point, and non-volatility [22]. They are classified according to their chemical composition as pure straight-chain alkanes and other hydrocarbons, most commonly called paraffin wax.

Wu et al. demonstrated the application of polystyrene encapsulated paraffin hybrid nanofluid for jet impingement and spray cooling. The paraffin molecules absorb heat during phase transfer and the polymer encapsulation prevents paraffin leakage and agglomeration. They reported that the polystyrene encapsulated paraffin hybrid nanofluid having 28 % vol. fraction enhances the heat transfer coefficient by 50 % and 70 % for jet impingement and spray cooling respectively compared to water [23]. Usually, low concentrations of polymer-encapsulated PCM-nanoparticle composite are preferred for the preparation of nanofluid for better dispersion stability and flow characteristics [24].

The purpose of the present study is to address the limitations of conventional heat transfer fluids and to explore the effects of dispersing GQD in paraffin wax, encapsulated using a suitable polymer. Composite nanoparticles consisting of GQD and paraffin granules are prepared initially. A random co-polymer 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) was used as shell material to encapsulate the graphene quantum dot-paraffin composite (GQD-P) using the emulsion polymerization method. The synthesis and characterization of GQD, GQD-P, and poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite (HEMA GQD-P) were reported in detail. Analysis of the thermo-physical properties of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid as a function of temperature and nanoparticle concentration were also reported in this work.

Paraffin wax (Melting point: 52–58 °C) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Chemicals such as hydroxyethylmethacrylate, citric acid, urea, copper chloride, and bipyridyl were purchased from Thermo-Fischer Scientific Limited, USA.

GQDs were prepared by using citric acid as a precursor and urea as a stabilizing agent as reported in our previous work [25]. Accordingly, 0.84 g citric acid and 072 g urea were dissolved properly in 20 ml deionized water. The formed solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal at 160 °C for 2 h for the pyrolysis of citric acid. During pyrolysis, the citric acid molecules undergo nucleation and get converted into partially graphene quantum dots suspension. The final dark brown solution which contains GQDs was dried using a vacuum drier at 80 °C overnight to produce graphene quantum dot particles. The schematic representation of the graphene quantum dot synthesis process is given in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite suspension
ce:section.ce:para: The nano-encapsulated GQD-P composite was synthesized by an emulsion polymerization method as represented in Fig. 2 . In this method, Pickering emulsion polymerization of hydroxyethyl methacrylate occurs on the surface of quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite particles in the presence of water and the emulsifying agent - bipyridyl. In emulsion polymerization, a thin flexible polymer made up of hydroxyethyl methacrylate is coated on the surface of a quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite particle core. The composite PCM was prepared by dispersing 0.5 g GQD particles in 5 g molten paraffin wax (1:10 wt. ratio) in a beaker. The GQD-P composite was then mixed with 5 g HEMA and 20 ml de-ionized water using an ultrasonicator at 65 °C for 1 h. The resultant suspension was sonicated for 30 min after adding 50 mg CuCl2, 160 mg bipyridyl, and 100 ml DI water. Nitrogen gas was purged in the solution for 10 min to maintain an inert atmosphere for the polymerization of HEMA. The solution was kept at 65 °C and homogenized continuously at 400 rpm for 2 h to obtain HEMA GQD-P nanocomposites suspension. In addition to ease of water dispersibility, HEMA-containing water-absorbable shells also exhibit enhanced thermal conductivity from 0.1 to 0.49 W/(mK) at 25 °C in the wet state compared with the dry capsule. The capsules show good durability by displaying no significant change in thermal properties and water dispersibility even after 10 heating/cooling cycles.

The morphological characterization of graphene quantum dots and HEMA coated GQD-P nanocomposite were carried out by transmission electron microscopy - TEM (JEM 2100 E Microscope, Japan). The luminescence property and the existence of quantum confinement effect in GQD particles were analyzed using a photoluminescence spectrophotometer (L 55 Photoluminescence Spectrophotometer, Perkin Elmer, USA). The average size and the topography of the HEMA GQD-P composite were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Regulus SU 6600, FE-SEM, Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). The structural fingerprint of the GQD nanoparticles and paraffin particles were analyzed by an infrared spectroscopy method - FTIR (Cary 630-, Agilent Technologies., US). The presence of graphene in GQD nanoparticles were characterized using Raman spectroscopy (iXR Raman Spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The thermal conductivity of GQD nanoparticles, paraffin PCM, and HEMA polymer was measured using (KD2 Pro, Decagon, USA). The latent heat capacity of the paraffin and HEMA present in the nanocomposite and the thermal stability of the HEMA GQD-P composite particles were analyzed using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC Q-20A/2000, TA Instruments, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: The nano-encapsulated GQD-P composite nanofluid was prepared at various concentrations (0 vol% - 6 vol%) at pH 7 by dispersing the required amount of composite nanoparticles in DI water using a probe ultrasonicator. The dispersion stability of the composite nanofluid was analyzed by a zeta potential analyzer. The thermal conductivity of the composite nanofluid was measured using a transient method thermal analyzer (KD2 Pro, Decagon, USA). The viscosity of the HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid was measured using a rheometer (Anton Paar-MCR-500, Germany). The surface tension of the HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid was measured using a surface tension analyzer (SEO, Korea). All analyses were carried out at the hybrid nanofluid concentrations ranging from 0 vol% to 6 vol%.

The existence of graphene quantum dots in the sample can be confirmed by its quantum confinement effect when it gets exposed to ultraviolet radiation. The excitation wavelength required for the photoemission of GQD due to the quantum confinement effect was determined by a UV absorption spectroscopy (Lambda 650, Perkin Elmer, USA) and the result is given in Fig. 3 . The result shows that the GQD has good absorbance in the range of 300 nm to 350 nm with a maximum absorbance at a wavelength λmax = 337 nm.

The photoluminescence emission of GQD-water suspension was taken at various excitation wavelengths (300 nm – 350 nm), and the emission spectra are shown in Fig. 4 . From the results, it was observed that the GQD suspension emits radiations in the wavelength ranging from 420 nm to 480 nm, which is in the visible region. This confirms the quantum confinement effect of the GQD particles and the photographic image of DI water and GQD suspension under UV light is given in Fig. 4 (inside) which shows that the material emits blue light when UV radiation falls on it. This closely resembles the emission wavelength of the GQD suspension in the photoluminescence spectrum.

The FTIR spectra of GQD nanoparticles and paraffin molecules are shown in Fig. 5 (a). In the GQD spectrum, the peaks obtained at 1595 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 are due to the existence of CC bond stretches in the graphene layer. The sharp peaks identified at 1195 cm−1 signify the occurrence of CC bond vibrations in graphene. The peak observed at 1715 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of CO stretches in carboxylic acid groups present in the GQD. A wide peak in the range of 2800 cm−1 - 3600 cm−1 is attributed to the OH vibration present in various functional groups in GQD nanoparticles [26].

In the paraffin spectrum, the peaks obtained at 721 cm−1 and 1467 cm−1 correspond to the existence of methyl – CH and methylene – CH2 rocking respectively in the paraffin molecules. Similarly, the presence of methylene – CH asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations in paraffin was confirmed by the peaks obtained at 2848 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 respectively [27]. The FTIR spectrum of HEMA GQD-P composite nanoparticles is given in Fig. 5 (b). HEMA spectrum gives a characteristic peak of CO at 2364 cm−1 and the peak at 1616 cm−1 is due to absorption of CC methacrylate vibration. The peaks in the range 1387–1470 cm−1 displays asymmetrical methyl bending (CH3) present in the polymeric HEMA. The peaks obtained in paraffin spectrum are also present in HEMA GQD-P FTIR spectrum. Typically, the peaks obtained at 720 cm−1 correspond to the existence of methyl – CH rocking in the paraffin molecules. Similarly, the presence of methylene – CH asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations in paraffin molecules are corresponds to the peaks obtained at 2840 cm−1 and 2915 cm−1 respectively [28].

Raman spectrum of the graphene quantum dot nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 6 . The intense peak identified at 1519 cm−1 (D band) corresponds to the breathing mode of k point phonons of A1g symmetry arise due to the CC vibrations in graphene structure. The spectrum is characterized by the G band peak observed at 1785 cm−1 resembles the E2g phonon of the sp2 carbon atoms present in graphene quantum dots. The intensity ratio of these two bands (Id/Ig) is low (1.08) which indicates the purity of graphene [29].

The TEM image of graphene quantum dot particles is given in Fig. 7 (a). An average size of 10–15 nm was observed for GQD nanoparticles. The quantum dots show spherical structures with even surface morphology with slight agglomeration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HEMA GQD-P composite particles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 (b) shows the SEM images of HEMA-encapsulated graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite particles The HEMA GQD-P composite nanoparticles exhibit even spherical structures with an average diameter of 100–120 nm. Fig. 8 shows the TEM image of HEMA GQD-P composite nanoparticles. It is evident from the image that the composite nanoparticle possess spherical core shell structure with HEMA layer encased the composite GQD-P complex. The HEMA polymer layer is clearly visible as a light shaded layer around the dark GQD-P spherical region. It is also evident from the TEM image that the HEMA layer is strongly bonded as a shell outside the GQD-P core even after a vigorous ultrasonication in ethanol during the TEM sample preparation.

The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) results of 1:10 wt. ratio (GQD: Paraffin) HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles is given in Fig. 9 . In this analysis, heating of HEMA GQD-P composite nanoparticles was done at 25 °C to 130 °C and it was observed a small dip at 48–50 °C. This is due to the solid-solid phase transition in which the closely packed paraffin molecules become random by absorbing heat. When the temperature is further increased, the paraffin molecules become more vibrant by absorbing heat and melt into the liquid phase by the solid-liquid phase transition [30]. At 55.79 °C onset temperature, the melting of paraffin wax starts and it completely melts at 73.87 °C. The latent heat of fusion is calculated by measuring the area under the reverse peak of the DSC curve. The latent heat capacity of paraffin molecules in the HEMA GQD-P composite was found to be 199.3 kJ/kg. The high heat capacity of paraffin in nanocomposite indicates that the material can absorb or release a significant amount of heat per unit mass without undergoing temperature change [31]. The material can effectively regulate temperatures in applications such as thermal energy storage systems, building materials, or electronic devices [32].

A reverse peak is also observed at an onset temperature of 104.61 °C which closely matches with the meting point of poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate and corresponds to the solid-liquid transition of HEMA in nanocomposite. Therefore, it was concluded that the molten paraffin is safely encased inside the solid shell of HEMA up to 104.61 °C and the nanocomposite can be used in any heat transfer applications till the melting point of HEMA.

The effect of weight ratio (GQD: paraffin wax) on the melting point and latent heat capacity of the HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles is given in Fig. 10 . It was observed from the results that there is no significant change in the phase change temperature of the composite nanoparticles by increasing the GQD loading. It was also found that the latent heat capacity of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite increases with an increase in GQD loading up to a 1:10 weight ratio and then starts to decrease with further addition. A maximum latent heat capacity enhancement of 24 % (199.3 kJ/kg) was obtained for 1:10 weight ratio HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles compared to HEMA-paraffin wax nanoparticles. The change in latent heat capacity is due to the two opposite factors. The increase in latent heat capacity of composite nanoparticles up to 1:10 wt. ratio is due to the interaction and intermolecular forces between graphene quantum dots and paraffin molecules. The decrease in latent heat capacity of the composite by further addition of GQD particles is due to the low latent heat capacity of GQD compared to pure PCM [33]. Therefore, the optimized nanocomposite HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite (1:10 wt. ratio) was used for all nanofluid preparation and analysis throughout this work.

The effect of GQD wt. ratio on the thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite is also shown in Fig. 10. The thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite found to get increases with an increase in GQD concentration. A maximum of 97.68 % enhancement in thermal conductivity was observed for 1.5:10 (GQD-P) nanocomposites compared to pure paraffin. From the results, it is evident that the high thermal conductivity of graphene quantum dots is mainly attributed to a relatively high thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite compared to pure paraffin. The general thermal properties of the GQD, paraffin PCM, and HEMA polymer observed are given in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermo-physical properties of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid

ce:section.ce:para: The dispersion stability of the HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid was analyzed using the zeta potential method at different pH conditions (2 to12) and the results are given in Fig. 11 . The zeta potential of composite nanofluid was observed to be 42 mV at pH 7, which possess high dispersion stability and shows its non-corrosive nature [34]. Therefore, HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid at pH 7 was used for all further analysis and application in this research. The magnitude of zeta potential was found to decrease when the nanofluid pH increased from 4 to 10. The formation of numerous OH- ions in the suspension increases the negatively charged ion concentration in the layer around the dispersed HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite particles which leads to a decrease in zeta potential [35]. The high dispersion stability of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid at pH 7 was confirmed by a sedimentation photographic method as shown in Fig. 12 . The result reveals that the nanofluid was highly stable without any agglomeration after 30 days of preparation.

The effect of HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite concentration on the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid is given in Fig. 13 (a). It was observed that the thermal conductivity of nanofluid increases with nanocomposite loading. A maximum of 75 % increase in thermal conductivity was observed for 6 vol% hybrid nanofluid at 30 °C. The high thermal conductivity of graphene quantum dots and the high latent heat capacity of paraffin are attributed to a significant increase in the thermal conductivity of composite nanofluid [36]. The HEMA and paraffin material fill in the gaps and imperfections at the interface between the solid GQD particles and water which reduces the thermal interface resistance and contributes to the overall increase in thermal conductivity [37].

The modified Maxwell model is generally considered as an analogy between the thermal conductivity of composite nanofluids considering two particles and mono disperse nanofluid which can be expressed as given in [38]. The modified Maxwell model given in eq. (1) can be used for the determination of theoretical thermal conductivity of mono and hybrid nanofluid.

are the effective thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid and thermal conductivity of the base fluid respectively, p1 and p2 represents particle 1 and 2 respectively.

are the thermal conductivities of particle 1 and 2 respectively.

Fig. 13 (b) shows the effect of temperature (30 °C – 80 °C) on the thermal conductivity of HEMA GQD-P hybrid nanofluid. It was found that the thermal conductivity of nanofluid increases with an increase in temperature. The thermal energy of the fluid increases with an increase in temperature, leading to greater Brownian motion of composite nanoparticles. This increased motion helps in better dispersion of particles in the fluid, reducing agglomeration, and thereby enhancing thermal conductivity [39].

The effect of composite nanoparticle concentration on the dynamic viscosity and surface tension of the nanofluid is given in Fig. 14 . The results disclose that in low concentrations (0 vol% - 1 vol%) of nanoparticles, the nanofluid viscosity may show a minimal increase compared to the base fluid. At this condition, the nanoparticles are relatively dispersed and their impact on the overall viscosity is inadequate [40]. On the other hand, as the concentration of nanoparticles increases from 1 vol% to 6 vol%, the viscosity of the hybrid nanofluid also increases exponentially. This is because the nanoparticles start to interact with each other, leading to increased resistance to flow. The formation of nanoparticle clusters or agglomerates also contributed to the enhancement in dynamic viscosity [41].

Fig. 14 also reveals the effect of nanoparticle loading on the surface tension of a hybrid nanofluid. In many cases, heat transfer fluids are designed to have surface tension values that promote efficient wetting and heat transfer rates. Surface tension is the tension of the surface film of a liquid caused by the attraction of the molecules in the surface layer by the bulk of the liquid, which tends to reduce its surface area. A good heat transfer fluid should possess a high value of surface tension [42]. The surface tension of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid was observed to enhance with an increase in nanoparticle loading. The high surface tension of nanofluid enhances its properties such as effective wetting, flow, pumpablility, minimized air entrapment, etc. This may be due to two reasons (i) agglomeration effect and (ii) disruption of base fluid molecules. As the nanoparticle loading in base fluid increases, the force of attraction between the particles at the liquid-air interface improves due to the decrease in space between them. This agglomeration can affect the interactions between the nanoparticles and the fluid molecules at the top surface. The presence of larger agglomerates may lead to an increase in surface tension due to changes in the fluid-solid interface. Otherwise, the addition of HEMA GQD-P composite nanoparticles modifies the molecular structure of the base fluid molecules near the surface. At higher concentrations, the nanoparticles may disrupt the default structure of the base fluid molecules at the interface, leading to a rise in surface tension [43].

Temperature influences the viscosity of the hybrid nanofluid and the results are shown in Fig. 15 . The viscosity of the composite nanofluid decreases with an increase in temperature. At higher temperatures, the thermal energy of the nanofluid system increases. This increased energy overcomes the attractive forces between composite nanoparticles dispersed in fluid, thus reducing the viscosity [44]. Lower viscosity allows for better movement and dispersion of nanoparticles, contributing to improved thermal conductivity as discussed earlier.

Similar to the viscosity, the surface tension of the HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluid was also found to decrease with an increase in temperature as represented in Fig. 16 . An average of 5.5 % (±1) decrease in surface tension was observed for the nanofluids when the temperature changes from 30 °C to 80 °C. This is because at higher temperatures, the thermal energy of the nanocomposite increases, leading to greater molecular motion and reduced cohesive forces at the nanofluid surface. As a result, the ability of the nanofluid to resist external forces and form a stable surface film decreases, causing a decrease in the surface tension of the nanofluid [45].

A comparison of the properties of present HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite and HEMA GQD-P hybrid nanofluid with the innovative similar material reported is given in Table 2 . It was found that the HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite and HEMA GQD-P hybrid nanofluid are showing promising heat transfer properties as compared to other similar innovative composite materials.

Uncertainty analysis is a crucial aspect of experimental research which helps in determining the precision of the experimental measurements. It provides a quantitative measure of how well the results can be reproduced under the same conditions. Uncertainty analysis provides a realistic understanding of the limitations and potential errors associated with experimental data [47]. The accuracy of the measured data was confirmed by uncertainty analysis using the standard method of propagation of errors as reported in our previous work [25]. The instruments, experimental parameters, derived parameters, and the respective uncertainties are given in Table 3 . It was observed that the maximum values of all the parametric uncertainties were <5 %, which shows the reliability of experimental data and equipment.

The HEMA GQD-P nanocomposite reported in this work possesses synergistic effect that enhances their potential as hybrid nanofluids with enhanced thermo-physical properties. However, they also come with several limitations that can impact their practical applications in thermal management systems. The preparation and stabilization of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluids is difficult that add to the overall cost, making them less economically viable for large-scale applications. The addition of composite nanoparticles beyond a certain limit increases the viscosity of the base fluid, leading to higher pumping power requirements. Understanding the complex interactions between HEMA GQD-P nanocomposites and the basefluid observed to be difficult and it requires advanced characterization techniques that are resource-intensive [48].

To overcome current limitations and optimize the use of HEMA GQD-P composite nanofluids in practical applications, several future recommendations can be considered. Developing cost-effective synthesis methods and scalable production techniques to facilitate large-scale manufacturing without compromising quality.to make them more affordable for widespread use. It is also recommended encapsulate the nanocomposite using other potential materials with enhanced thermal stability in order to prepare hybrid nanofluids that maintain stability and performance at high temperatures, essential for applications in high-heat environments.

In this work, a hybrid nanofluid by dispersing poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-encased graphene quantum dot-paraffin nanocomposite (HEMA GQD-P) in water was prepared. The nanocomposite was synthesized by mixing graphene quantum dots of size (10 to 15 nm) with paraffin wax nanoparticles and coated using HEMA by Pickering emulsion polymerization reaction. The synthesized GQD exhibits the quantum confinement effect emitting visible light at 420–440 nm under UV excitation. The latent heat capacity of the nanocomposite was found get increase with an increase in GQD loading up to 1:10 (GQD: paraffin wt.) ratio. A maximum of 21.8 % enhancement in latent heat capacity was obtained for GQD-P nanocomposite compared to pure paraffin. The nanocomposite material can absorb heat during periods of excess and release it during periods of demand, maintaining a relatively constant temperature. The thermal properties of HEMA GQD-P hybrid nanofluid were also enhanced due to the synergistic effect of GQD with high thermal conductivity and paraffin with high latent heat capacity. A maximum thermal conductivity enhancement of 53 % (±2) was obtained for the nanofluid at 0.3 vol% concentration at 30 °C. The dispersion stability was analyzed at various pH conditions (2−12) using the zeta potential method and the nanofluid was found to be highly stable at pH 7 with a zeta potential of +42.5 mV. The viscosity and surface tension of the hybrid nanofluid also increase with an increase in nanoparticle concentration. Uncertainty analysis of the experiments reveals that the maximum values of all the parametric uncertainties were <5 %, which shows the reliability of experimental data. This novel combination of nanoparticle and PCM offers the potential to significantly enhance the thermal properties of nanofluids, which leads to a more efficient and sustainable thermal management system and paves the way for better heat transfer technologies.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Poly-hydroxyethyl methacrylate Graphene Quantum Dot-paraffin nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:para: Vishnuprasad Selvaraj: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. S. Sreelekshmi: Visualization, Investigation. Mariya Binoy: Methodology, Formal analysis. Sony George: Writing – review & editing. Karthick Senthilkumar: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111295.
